Proposal title: Mapping Peace: A GIS-Based Storytelling Platform for Global Peace Education from Hiroshima
Abstract:
This research develops interactive peace education platforms integrating GIS with
storytelling functions. In 2025, all atomic bomb–related sites within five kilometers of
Hiroshima’s hypocenter were mapped on Re:Earth. By layering historical and global data,
the platform encourages users worldwide to reflect on peace and their own
responsibilities.
Description:
This research aims to create an interactive digital platform that encourages people
 around the world engaged in peace education to explore the meaning of peace from multiple perspectives and discover new values related to it. By integrating geographic
information systems (GIS), storytelling, and multimedia resources, the platform seeks to
 make learning about war, nuclear weapons, and peace more accessible, engaging, and
 personally relevant. Our ultimate goal is to encourage users not only to learn about the
 past but also to reflect on their own attitudes and future actions toward peace.
A key feature of this research is the use of GIS technology, which allows different types of
 information to be layered on a digital map, enabling users to visually understand spatial
 relationships and compare information from different places at a glance. This method is
particularly effective for peace education because it connects historical events to specific
 locations and helps users comprehend complex global issues through spatial
 perspectives.
In 2025, we mapped all known atomic bomb–related sites located within a
five-kilometer radius of the hypocenter in Hiroshima using the Web-GIS platform Re:Earth.
These include approximately ninety-six monuments and remains that preserve the
 memories and realities of the devastation caused by the atomic bomb. Currently, we are
 expanding the spatial scope of the project and increasing the amount and diversity of
 information on the platform. By layering a wider range of datasets, we aim to strengthen
the platform’s role as an information-rich resource for peace education.
Storytelling also plays a central role in the platform. Through personal narratives, users
 can experience the lives of individuals connected to the atomic bombing and gain a
 deeper emotional understanding of historical events. Many people feel that topics related
 to war or nuclear weapons are distant or difficult to understand. By presenting information
 through narratives and interactive media, we aim to make peace education more relatable
 and meaningful.
As part of this research, we conducted interviews with fifty-five individuals from
seventeen countries, including the United States, France, Germany, and the Philippines,
and incorporated their messages for peace into the platform. We also created narrative
 content about three individuals connected to the atomic bombing: a third-generation
 atomic bomb survivor who shares messages of peace through dance while conveying her
 grandmother’s testimony; Eizo Nomura, a survivor exposed very close to the hypocenter
 who later suffered from the aftereffects of radiation; and Chiyo Miyazaki, the daughter of a
 Korean second-generation survivor, whose story highlights the experiences of foreign
forced laborers who were also victims of the bombing.
These stories are presented through maps, photographs, videos, and text, alongside
 visual features such as three-dimensional models showing the number of nuclear
 weapons possessed by each country and augmented reality reconstructions of the
 Nakajima district before the bombing. Furthermore, we are developing concrete peace
 education curricula that utilize this GIS platform as a learning tool.

Abstract: An “Atomic Bomb Drawings” project in which high school students in Hiroshima create paintings based on survivors’ testimonies. We present it as an interactive, map-based digital archive. Using the open-source data platform Re:Earth, we showcase the visualizations and the overall initiative in a poster presentation.
Description:
This session introduces a GIS and digital archive visualization project centered on "Atomic Bomb Drawings," led by the Hidenori Watanabe Laboratory, Graduate School of Interdisciplinary Information Studies, The University of Tokyo, in collaboration with high school students from Hiroshima and Nagasaki. The "Atomic Bomb Drawings" are a collection of artworks created by students at Hiroshima City Motomachi High School (General Course and Creative Expression Course). Through collaboration between atomic bomb survivors and students, the students carefully interview survivors about the scenes from the time of the bombing that remain in their memories and depict them in paintings. This ongoing initiative aims to convey the circumstances of that time. As atomic bomb survivors continue to age, this initiative aims to preserve the realities of the atomic bombing through paintings for future generations. Through the creative process, high school students inherit the thoughts and experiences of survivors, reflect on the value of peace, and carry these messages forward to the next generation.
This project goes beyond simply browsing these works in a list format, reconstructing them as an experience where they can be viewed and explored in connection with geographic information. By placing the Atomic Bomb Drawings on a digital map and connecting locations, paintings, and related explanatory information (metadata), we enable viewers to experience the works and data in a new way. Furthermore, we visualize insights that emerge from spatial relationships (such as memories concentrated in the same area, traces of movement, and the spread of damage), leading to understanding that is difficult to achieve through conventional search or list displays. Additionally, the collaboration between students from Hiroshima and Nagasaki (Nagasaki Higashi High School)  holds great significance as co-creation among young people from the two atomic-bombed cities.

Proposal title: Visualizing Nagasaki's Peace Sites, Trees, and AI-Colorized Photos with Re:Earth: From Passive Learning to Active Inquiry
Abstract:
Visualization of Atomic Bomb Ruins Using Web GIS “Re:Earth” and Its Application to Peace Education
― Nagasaki Higashi High School's Inquiry Utilizing Geospatial Information ―
Description: We are the Re:Earth team from Nagasaki Higashi High School. In this research project, we are investigating whether using the Re:Earth Web GIS platform to visualize the ruins of the atomic bomb on maps can increase interest in peace. In Nagasaki, the number of atomic bomb survivors is decreasing year by year as they grow older, and, passing on the memories of the bombing to the next generation has become a major challenge. Therefore, we aimed to build a learning environment that utilizes geospatial information to connect the history of the bombing to existing “places” for understanding. This research utilizes Re:Earth, a Web GIS platform developed by Eucarya Inc. Re:Earth is a tool capable of managing, transforming, and visualizing geospatial data, featuring a “storytelling function” that presents information sequentially as locations are traced. This technology is also utilized in initiatives such as Project PLATEAU, the Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism's urban data project. We utilized this feature to create a digital map viewable even from smartphones. In this study, we created the following three maps with the aim of integrating different types of historical data as geospatial information. First is the “A-bomb Ruins Map.” It organizes the atomic bomb-related ruins remaining in Nagasaki City onto a map, enabling a geographical understanding of the extent of the damage and the spatial relationships between sites. Second is the “A-bombed Trees Map.” Focusing on trees that survived the atomic bomb's devastation, we visualized their locations on a map. This aims to convey both nature's ability to recover and their significance as symbols of peace. Third is the “AI Colorized Photo Map.” Nagasaki Higashi High School is also conducting research activities to colorize pre-war black-and-white photographs of Nagasaki using AI. This map places photos created by the school’s AI colorization team onto a map, enabling users to connect current locations with the pre-war cityscape for better understanding. We have produced a total of six versions of these three kinds of maps, including Japanese and English versions. This was done in order to create an environment where people not only domestically but also internationally can learn about Nagasaki's history. Currently, with the cooperation of Professor Byungdug Jun from Nagasaki University's Faculty of Information and Data Science, we are working on an initiative to overlay aerial photographs taken before the atomic bombings onto maps. Furthermore, during the research process, we have also received technical advice from Professor Hidenori Watanabe of the Graduate School of Interdisciplinary Information Studies, University of Tokyo. Through these initiatives, we aim to evolve peace education from “passive learning based on reading materials” to “active learning where students explore and deepen their understanding.”

Proposal title: Reflections on the Sekishu Kaido Transportation in Depopulated Areas
Abstract:
Based on the research we’ve been conducting on the Sekishu Kaido in our seminar,
we’re exploring ways to improve transportation systems in depopulated areas
Description:
I'm thinking about transportation in depopulated areas through the Sekishu Kaido. Because my home is far from school, I realized I did not know much about the Oasa region. It is a snowy, mountainous area facing severe depopulation and an aging society. This inspired me to join a research seminar to learn about the region. Through this seminar, 
we first researched the geography and history of the Sekishu Kaido. It was an Edo-period trail where feudal lords walked about 40 km to move Edo. To see if walking 40 km a day was truly possible, we conducted fieldwork and walked a 13 km section of the trail. Using a map app to reconstruct the route, we spent 3.5 hours navigating steep, narrow, and poorly marked paths. This experience made me realize it is somehow possible to walk 40 km in a single dayimmense hardships past travelers faced with their traditional footwear and luggage.
While transportation historically evolved from walking to public transit, depopulated areas are now losing these vital transportation. Moving forward, I plan to research alternative transportation methods across Japan, examine public-private collaborations, and think from an administrative perspective. My ultimate goal is to propose a practical transportation system that allows residents in depopulated areas to move around easily.
